'%s' : '\\'+c)` matches each special character INDIVIDUALLY: a literal '%' becomes '\%' but the following 's' (not in the character class) is left untouched. `spawnAdb` uses `spawn('adb', args)` with no shell, so this exact string reaches the local `adb` binary unmangled; `adb shell` then joins the args and hands them to the DEVICE's shell (exactly as the code's own comment at lines 175-178 states), which strips a single unquoted backslash and passes through the literal next character. So `\%s` de-escapes on-device back to the literal two-char sequence `%s` — bit-for-bit identical to what the encoder emits for an actual space (line 185's `c===' ' ? '%s' : ...` branch). `input text` (per this file's own documented assumption of '%s' as its space token) can't tell the two apart and converts it to a single space. Concrete trigger: type("increase%special") -> escaped "increase\%special" -> device shell delivers "increase%special" to `input text` -> tool's own %s convention fires on the substring at index 8-9 -> renders as "increase pecial". No guard anywhere disambiguates an intentionally-escaped '%' from the tool's own space token once it survives the shell layer.
allRaw.suggestedFix: A single backslash cannot protect '%' because the device shell consumes exactly one level and `input text`'s %s→space substitution is applied to the final string with no escape awareness of its own. Detect any literal '%' in the original text immediately followed by 's' and break the `input text` call so the two characters never land in the same argument the tool scans (e.g. flush pending text, send 's' via a separate keyevent/input-text call, then resume) — or abandon the `input text` %s convention altogether in favor of a typing path (e.g. ADB Keyboard's base64 broadcast-intent method) that doesn't reinterpret arbitrary substrings of the payload.

allRaw.title: Screen dimensions are cached forever, so scroll() swipes to the wrong place after rotation or after a transient query failure
allRaw.reasoning: Confirmed directly: `this.screenDimsQueried = true;` (line 97) is set unconditionally BEFORE the try/catch that attempts the `wm size` query, and there is no code anywhere in the class that ever resets `screenDimsQueried` or re-queries dimensions. Two concrete triggers both hold up: (1) if `spawnAdb(['shell','wm','size'])` throws (e.g. ADB_TIMEOUT on a cold-started device), control falls to the catch block, and the hardcoded defaults 1080x2400 (lines 111-113) are cached permanently — screenDimsQueried is already true, so future calls short-circuit at line 94 and never retry even after the device becomes reachable with a genuinely different resolution. (2) once real dimensions are cached, an in-session orientation change (portrait<->landscape) is never detected or invalidated, so scroll()'s startX/startY/endY math (lines 193-203) keeps using stale values. No guard mitigates either path.
allRaw.suggestedFix: Only set `screenDimsQueried = true` after a successful parse (move it inside the `if (match)` block, or track success separately from 'attempted'), so a failed query is retried on the next call instead of permanently locking in the 1080x2400 fallback. Separately, consider exposing an explicit refresh/invalidate hook (or a short TTL) so a legitimate mid-session rotation can be detected rather than relying on a one-time cache for the life of the instance.

allRaw.title: pressKey() silently sends ENTER for any key it doesn't explicitly recognize
allRaw.reasoning: Confirmed exactly as described: `let code = '66';` is the default, and only 'enter' (line 210, redundantly re-set to '66'), 'back' (line 211, '4'), and 'home' (line 212, '3') override it. Any other key name — pressKey('tab'), pressKey('delete'), pressKey('escape'), pressKey('space'), etc. — falls through with `code` still '66' and the provider issues `input keyevent 66` (KEYCODE_ENTER) with no error, warning, or no-op path. Concrete trigger: an agent focused on a multi-line text field calls pressKey('escape') to dismiss a picker, or pressKey('tab') to move focus, and instead the field receives an Enter keypress, which for many forms triggers submission — a state change the caller never requested and that isn't traceable to a key-mapping gap without reading this source.
allRaw.suggestedFix: Add explicit mappings for other common keys the agent is likely to request (tab -> 61, delete/backspace -> 67, escape -> 111, space -> 62, arrows -> 19-22), and for a genuinely unrecognized key name, reject the promise or log a clear warning and skip sending a keyevent rather than silently substituting Enter.

allRaw.title: On-device dump/screenshot files leak when the adb pull step fails after a successful dump
allRaw.reasoning: Confirmed in both cited locations. In getUiHierarchyXml (lines 123-132): `await this.spawnAdb(['shell','uiautomator','dump',devXml]); await this.spawnAdb(['pull', devXml, xmlTempFile]); await this.spawnAdb(['shell','rm','-f',devXml]).catch(()=>{});` — if the dump succeeds but the pull call rejects (e.g. ADB_TIMEOUT, which spawnAdb explicitly models and rejects on, per lines 35-41), the `await` on the rejected pull promise throws immediately, jumping straight to the outer `catch { return ""; }` and skipping the `rm -f` line entirely, so devXml is never deleted. Same shape in getScreenContext (lines 234-241): the cleanup is chained via `.then()` after pull's `.then()`, so a pull rejection short-circuits the chain before the rm `.then()` runs. waitForUiIdle calls getUiHierarchyXml up to 10 times per idle-wait with a fresh UUID each time, so repeated transient pull failures do accumulate orphaned files with no cleanup path.
allRaw.suggestedFix: Wrap the dump+pull steps in a try/finally (or attach the `rm -f ... .catch(()=>{})` cleanup as a `.finally()`/always-run step) so the on-device temp file is removed regardless of whether the subsequent pull succeeds, in both getUiHierarchyXml and getScreenContext.

allRaw.title: waitForUiIdle() can treat repeated dump failures as "UI settled" and return early
allRaw.reasoning: Refuted by reading the actual condition at line 148: `if (currentXml && currentXml === lastXml)`. The `currentXml &&` guard means an empty string (which is what getUiHierarchyXml returns on any failure, line 131) is falsy and short-circuits the whole condition to false, so two consecutive failures do NOT increment stableCount — they instead hit the `else { stableCount = 0; }` branch each time, exactly like any other non-matching pair. The candidate's premise ("currentXml===''===lastXml is already true after just ONE poll, giving stableCount=1") is factually wrong: the guard explicitly prevents empty-string equality from ever counting as stability. A run of dump failures therefore keeps stableCount at 0 and the loop continues polling for the full maxPolls budget (10 iterations) rather than breaking early — the opposite of what's claimed.
allRaw.suggestedFix: No fix needed — the existing `currentXml &&` guard already prevents this failure mode.
